mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M440d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M440d (B57) engine typically costs £4,000–£6,500 supply only. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,500 depending on location and workshop rates. Main dealer new engines start from £14,000–£18,000+ supply only. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M440d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the B57 is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern diesel engines. It's described as 'the golden mean between performance, consumption and longevity' and 'probably one of the best engines BMW has ever produced'. Unlike the N47 and early N57 engines, B57 timing chain issues are 'exceedingly rare'. However, EGR cooler coolant leaks (subject to BMW recalls), DPF clogging from short journeys, carbon buildup, and injector wear are well-documented issues.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M440d?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main known issues are: (1) EGR cooler coolant leaks — the most prevalent problem, subject to global BMW recalls, (2) DPF clogging — particularly on vehicles used for short urban journeys, (3) Carbon buildup in the intake system — from oil vapour and soot, and (4) Injector wear — causing rough idle and misfires.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW M440d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £25,000–£40,000 M440d, a £5,000–£6,500 recon is 15–25% of the car's value — worth it given the M440d's strong retained values. For higher-mileage examples, evaluate the car's overall condition before committing. The B57's robust reliability means that with proper maintenance, these engines can achieve very high mileages.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M440d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M440d uses the B57D30T2 engine (also known as B57D30B) — a 3.0-litre twin-turbo inline-six diesel. The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Does the B57 have timing chain issues?

acceptedAnswer.text: Unlike the infamous N47 diesel, the B57's timing chain is notably robust and reliable. Timing chain stretch and guide failures are 'exceedingly rare'. The B57 timing chain is located at the rear of the engine but has been significantly improved over earlier BMW diesel engines. With proper maintenance and regular oil changes using the correct BMW LL-04 spec oil, timing chain issues are unlikely.

problems.title: EGR Cooler Coolant Leak
problems.symptoms: Low coolant warning, coolant loss with no visible external leak, white exhaust smoke, rough idling, burning smell from engine bay, engine warning light
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — a global recall affects BMW diesel engines including B57-equipped models
problems.repairCost: £0 (free under recall) / £600–£1,200 (out of warranty — EGR cooler replacement)
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: verify recall completion status immediately; coolant loss can cause serious engine damage

problems.recommendation: The EGR cooler is one of the most prevalent problems with the B57 engine. Coolant can seep into the intake system, causing white exhaust smoke, rough idling, or in worst cases, serious engine damage. BMW has issued multiple recalls affecting millions of diesel vehicles globally over EGR cooler faults that could cause coolant leaks and, in rare cases, fire risk. Verify recall completion before buying any M440d — this is a free fix at the dealership. If the vehicle is out of warranty and the recall wasn't performed, replacement costs can be significant.

failureLink.label: Read the full EGR cooler failure guide

problems.title: DPF Clogging (Urban Driving)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced performance, increased fuel consumption, "Drivetrain" warning message, limp mode
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — short urban journeys accelerate the issue
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)
problems.recommendation: Like all modern diesels, the M440d's DPF is sensitive to short urban journeys that prevent the engine from reaching optimal temperatures for regeneration. If you primarily drive short distances, factor in regular motorway runs to allow passive DPF regenerations. Ignoring DPF warnings can lead to clogged filters, requiring expensive replacement. Some owners report that driving the car hard on the motorway can clear minor blockages — but if the warning persists, book a forced regeneration at a specialist.

failureLink.label: Read the full DPF clogging guide

problems.title: Carbon Build-Up in Intake System
problems.symptoms: Reduced responsiveness, poorer fuel efficiency, hesitation on acceleration, rough idle
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–100,000 miles — more common with frequent short trips
problems.recommendation: If you're doing the majority of your driving in short distances or stop-and-go traffic, carbon buildup in the intake valves and manifold is a known issue. The engine may not reach the temperature needed to burn away oil vapour and soot, leading to clogging. Symptoms include reduced responsiveness, poorer fuel efficiency, and slower acceleration. Regular long journeys and using premium diesel can help mitigate this issue. If symptoms develop, an intake cleaning (walnut blasting) is the recommended solution.

failureLink.label: Read the full carbon buildup guide → /failures/carbon-buildup-intake/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Injector Wear
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfires, increased fuel consumption, black smoke from exhaust, engine warning light
problems.repairCost: £400–£800 per injector (new Bosch injectors from £406.74 each)
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)
problems.recommendation: The B57 uses Bosch common-rail diesel injectors that can wear over time. Symptoms include rough idle, misfires, and increased fuel consumption. A new Bosch injector for the B57D30B costs approximately £406.74 including VAT. If you experience rough running or black smoke, have the injectors tested and replaced as necessary. Catching injector wear early prevents more serious engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This M440d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with BMW-approved oil (5W-30 or 0W-30 LL-04)
* Confirmed EGR recall completion — this affects BMW diesel engines globally
* Evidence of regular motorway use (DPF health)
* Remaining manufacturer warranty — these are complex performance diesels
avoidIf: * No proof of EGR recall work — this is a free fix; lack of it suggests neglect
* Coolant loss with no documented fix — EGR cooler leaks are common
* Poor service history — the B57 is reliable but needs proper maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The M440d's B57 is one of BMW's finest modern diesel engines — a 3.0-litre twin-turbo inline-six with 340 bhp and 700 Nm that delivers effortless performance and genuine character. It's widely regarded as "the golden mean between performance, consumption and longevity" and "probably one of the best engines BMW has ever produced". The B57 is a significant improvement over the N57 that preceded it — timing chain issues are "exceedingly rare". However, EGR cooler recalls, DPF sensitivity, carbon buildup, and injector wear are well-documented weaknesses. Buy with full service history, confirmed recall work, and evidence of regular long-distance driving — and you'll have one of the most rewarding performance diesels on the road.

cta.label: → Compare M440d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW M440d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * EGR Recall — Verify EGR cooler recall completion — this is critical and affects BMW diesel engines globally
* Cold Start — Listen for any unusual rattling or knocking (B57 timing chain is generally robust, but listen anyway)
* DPF Health — Check for DPF warning lights; short journeys are a red flag
* Service History — Including the B57 engine and confirmed BMW-approved oil changes (5W-30 / 0W-30 LL-04)
* Coolant Level — Check for frequent top-ups; inspect for EGR cooler leaks
* Injector Health — Any rough idle, misfires, or black smoke?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Look for EGR, DPF, or injector-related codes
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
